title: Large value estimates in number theory, harmonic analysis, and computer science

def:2.1.summary: The large-value count: for a $T\times N$ matrix $M$, $\mathrm{LV}_{M,\ell^p}(\lambda):=\max_{\|v\|_p=N^{1/p}}\#\{i:|(Mv)_i|>\lambda\}$. The single quantity unifying the large-value problem across number theory (Dirichlet polynomials), Fourier analysis (restriction/Strichartz), and statistics/CS (planted-vector detection).

prop:3.1.summary: Sharpness example: for any $U\subset\{1,\dots,T\}$ with $|U|\sim N^{2-2\sigma}$ one can choose $|b_n|\lessapprox1$ with $|(M_{\mathrm{Dir}}b)_t|\gtrsim N^\sigma$ for $t\in U$. Shows the Montgomery bound $N^{2-2\sigma}$ cannot be beaten — the lower bound the whole problem aims at.

prop:3.2.summary: Random matrices: a random $T\times N$ matrix (entries from suitable distributions) obeys, with high probability, the conjectured large-value estimate. Evidence that the truth for $M_{\mathrm{Dir}}$ should be the Montgomery bound, not something weaker.

conjecture:3.3.summary: Montgomery's large value conjecture: for $\sigma>\tfrac12$, $\mathrm{LV}_{M_{\mathrm{Dir}},\ell^\infty}(N^\sigma)\lessapprox N^{2-2\sigma}$ — i.e. a Dirichlet polynomial $D(t)=\sum_{n=N}^{2N}b_ne^{it\log n}$ ($|b_n|\le1$) exceeds $N^\sigma$ on $\lessapprox N^{2-2\sigma}$ unit-separated points.

conjecture:3.4.summary: Montgomery's $\ell^q$ conjecture: $\|M_{\mathrm{Dir}}\|_{\infty\to q}\lessapprox N+N^{1/2}T^{1/q}$ for every $q\ge2$. The mean-value (rather than large-value) form; equivalent to conjecture:3.3.

prop:5.1.summary: Orthogonality / operator-norm method: $\sum_{t=1}^T|D(t)|^2\lesssim T\sum_n|b_n|^2$ for $T\ge N$. The $L^2$ baseline (Plancherel), giving the trivial large-value bound.

prop:5.2.summary: Power method: for even $q$ and $\sum_n|b_n|^2\le N$, $\sum_{t=1}^T|\sum_{n\sim N}b_ne^{it\log n}|^q\lessapprox N^q+N^{q/2}T$. The high-moment refinement of the $L^2$ bound.

prop:5.3.summary: The Montgomery–Halász $MM^*$ method: if every off-diagonal entry of $MM^*$ has norm $\lessapprox N^{1/2}$, then $|(Mb)_t|\ge N^\sigma$ on a controlled set. The duality/spectral method behind most classical large-value theorems — and the one Guth–Maynard refines via the Schatten tensor.

prop:5.4.summary: The $\sigma\le\tfrac34$ obstruction: for $\tfrac12\le\sigma\le\tfrac34$ there is a function $\tilde D$ and set $W$ with $N^{2\sigma}|W|\sim TN$ — an 'almost counterexample' showing why the $\sigma\le\tfrac34$ regime is hard and where the classical methods stall.

thm:6.1.summary: The Guth–Maynard theorem (= Theorem 1.1 of [GM]): for $T=N^{6/5}$ and $\sigma\le\tfrac34$, a new large-value bound for $M_{\mathrm{Dir}}$ — proved via the 'Schatten tensor', i.e. a cubic-trace ($\mathrm{tr}((MM^*)^3)$) refinement of the $MM^*$ method that exploits the multiplicative structure of $\{e^{it\log n}\}$. The first improvement over the classical methods in this regime.

thm:6.2.summary: Random-matrix benchmark (Theorem 4.4 of [DHPT]): for $N\le T\le N^2$ and a Gaussian $T\times N$ matrix, a polynomial-strength large-value bound holds whp. Calibrates how close thm:6.1 gets to the 'generic' truth.

prop:7.1.summary: The no-additive-structure case: if $\Phi\subset2\pi\mathbb{Z}$ so $D$ is a Fourier series on $\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$, then $\int_0^1|D(t)|^{2s}dt$ is bounded by the major-arc contribution — Vinogradov-mean-value-flavoured bound when the frequencies carry no extra additive structure.

prop:7.2.summary: The discrete companion of prop:7.1: $\sum_{t_1,t_2\in\mathcal{T}}|D(t_1-t_2)|^{2s}$ bounded by the major-arc term. The form relevant to large-value estimates over discrete point sets.

conjecture:7.3.summary: The conjectured optimal second-moment large-value (Heath-Brown-type) bound: for $D(t)=\sum_{n=N}^{2N}b_ne^{it\log n}$ ($|b_n|\le1$) and a $1$-separated $\mathcal{T}$, $\sum_{t_1,t_2\in\mathcal{T}}|D(t_1-t_2)|^2\le T^{o(1)}(|\mathcal{T}|N^2+|\mathcal{T}|^2N+\cdots)$. The exact form the trace method aims to reach.

thm:8.1.summary: Sum-of-squares strength (Theorem 1.5 of [DHPT]): for a random $T\times N$ matrix and $\sigma>1-\alpha/4$, the SOS hierarchy at degree $O(1)$ certifies $|W|\lesssim N^{\alpha+1-2\sigma-c}$. The algorithmic upper end.

conjecture:8.2.summary: Computational-complexity barrier ([DHPT]): for $\sigma\le1-\alpha/4$, the degree-$O(1)$ SOS method *cannot* prove $|W|\lesssim N^{\cdots-c}$. A conjectural limit on a whole class of proof techniques — including, plausibly, the trace method.

thm:8.3.summary: Planted-vs-random distinguishing ([MW], Theorem 4.5): for $1<\alpha<2$, $\tfrac12<\sigma<1$, $\sigma>1-\alpha/4$, the planted-vector and pure-random models are statistically distinguishable. Ties the large-value problem to a well-studied detection problem in statistics.

conjecture:9.1.summary: A Kakeya-type lower bound: for $d\ge2$ and tube/dual-tube pairs $(\theta,\theta^*)$, $|\bigcup_\theta(\theta^*+x_\theta)|\ge C_{\epsilon,d}^{-1}\cdots$ for any translates $x_\theta$. A geometric obstruction: if true it would limit how much the harmonic-analysis side of the large-value problem can be improved.

edges.note: The Guth–Maynard bound is a cubic-trace ('Schatten tensor') refinement of the Montgomery–Halász $MM^*$ method.
The $MM^*$ method generalizes the bare $L^2$ orthogonality bound by tracking the off-diagonal of $MM^*$.
The power method is the high-even-moment version of the $L^2$ bound.
The trace method splits according to the additive structure of the frequency set, using the structured-case estimates of §7.
Discrete version of the Fourier-series moment bound.
The 'almost counterexample' shows the $MM^*$ method alone cannot reach the conjecture for $\sigma\le\tfrac34$ — the reason a refinement is needed.
Establishes a partial form of the conjectured optimal second-moment large-value bound.
Progress toward Montgomery's large value conjecture in the hard regime.
The $\ell^q$ mean-value form is equivalent to the large-value form.
Shows the conjectured exponent $N^{2-2\sigma}$ is best possible — it is a lower bound, not just an upper-bound target.
Random matrices achieve the conjectured bound — heuristic evidence for $M_{\mathrm{Dir}}$.
The SOS barrier is the negative companion of the SOS achievability result.
Both come from the planted-vector / spiked-matrix model of statistics.
The random-matrix benchmark is a quantitative form of the heuristic that random matrices behave well.
If the Kakeya-type lower bound holds, the harmonic-analysis route to the large-value conjecture faces a geometric obstruction.

the_large_value_problem.description: The unifying quantity $\mathrm{LV}_{M,\ell^p}(\lambda)$, the Montgomery conjectures (large-value and $\ell^q$ forms), the sharpness example, and the random-matrix heuristic.

classical_methods.description: Orthogonality / operator-norm, the power method, the Montgomery–Halász $MM^*$ method, and the $\sigma\le\tfrac34$ obstruction that stalls them.

trace_method_and_structure.description: The Guth–Maynard Schatten-tensor (cubic-trace) refinement, the random-matrix benchmark, and the structured/unstructured-frequency special cases it splits along.

barriers.description: Limits on the problem: the sum-of-squares achievability/lower-bound pair, the planted-vector detection connection, and a Kakeya-type geometric obstruction.

GM.title: New large value estimates for Dirichlet polynomials

DHPT.title: Sum-of-squares lower bounds for the Dirichlet-polynomial large value problem (and random matrix variants)

Halasz:1969.title: Über die Mittelwerte multiplikativer zahlentheoretischer Funktionen

BourgainDemeter:l2.title: The proof of the l^2 Decoupling Conjecture
